original_abstract:
The policing of sexual assault is currently in a state of chronic crisis. This book investigates why police investigations consistently fail most survivors and proposes a new framework for fundamental structural change. Despite decades of research, policy adjustments, and legal reforms intended to bridge the "rape justice gap," conviction rates remain dismal and the investigative process continues to be an arduous ordeal for victims.

Recognizing that police institutions are notoriously resistant to reform—even amidst a collapse in public trust—this work argues that profound change requires a departure from traditional approaches. Rather than asking what it is about the crime of rape that makes it difficult to investigate, the authors shift the lens to ask what it is about the culture and structure of the police that makes it so difficult for them to investigate rape effectively. *Policing Rape* introduces a practice-oriented theoretical framework designed to radically and sustainably transform investigations, prevention strategies, and the quality of officer engagement with survivors. Drawing on the authors' experiences implementing these changes within Operation Soteria Bluestone, this book offers a roadmap for the radical transformation of rape policing and is essential reading for anyone committed to improving justice outcomes.
humanized_abstract:
Sexual Assault and Policing: Understanding the Complexity of Rape Justice An unprecedented analysis of the crisis in policing sexual assault and how to bring about change This book examines the permanent state of crisis in the policing of sexual assault. It delves into the complex issues and examines their relationships and the need for a fundamental rethink of how policing sexual assault can be undertaken in a way that delivers improvements in justice for survivors. Over decades of research, policy reforms and changes to the law aimed at closing the "rape justice gap" have done nothing to improve low rates of convictions and make an already stressful process for victims harder to go through.

While it may seem straightforward to argue for reform of police institutions, these institutions have proven, time and again, to be resistant to change in the most ingrained of ways, most obviously following periods of extreme backlash from the public. So where do we start? What is it about rape or the crime of rape, that makes it so difficult to investigate when clearly other crimes are also investigated as thoroughly if not more thoroughly? The answer is quite simple, in fact, and it isn’t at all to do with the crime of rape. Instead, the culture and practices of policing must be viewed as a potential barrier in the investigation of rape as well as being a factor in the dynamics of preventing rape as well as improving the way in which the police engage with survivors. Policing Rape offers an innovative practice oriented theoretical perspective on policing rape that not only outlines methods for bringing about radical, sustainable change to the investigation of sexual assault as well as preventative work in this area but does so with a completely unique set of practices based on the authors’ ground-breaking work of Operation Soteria Bluestone. Read this book if you want to contribute to the creation of real, tangible change for victims and for a safer, better society for all.
